1: The parable discourse (Matthew 13) occurs immediately after the Beelzebul controversy and the request for a sign — in the context of official rejection. The shift to parabolic teaching at this point (Matthew 13:13: because seeing they do not see) is a narrative turning point: the kingdom's mysteries are now disclosed selectively, revealed to those who receive Jesus and concealed from those who reject him. In rabbinic tradition, parables (meshalim) were a standard teaching form for making abstract truths accessible — Ezekiel, Hosea, and Nathan used mashal-forms. But Jesus's parables function simultaneously as revelation (for disciples) and concealment (for hardened hearers), a dual function unprecedented in the tradition.
2: The crowd gathered by the sea (para tēn thalassan) was so large that Jesus boarded a boat and sat in it while teaching from the water — the boat’s deck serving as an elevated platform visible to the crowd on the shore. The natural amphitheater formed by the curved shoreline at Capernaum/Gennesaret would have provided excellent acoustics for a teacher speaking from a boat a short distance offshore. This was an outdoor teaching occasion of unusual scale, distinct from synagogue teaching.
3: Parables (meshalim) were a standard Jewish teaching form: a short narrative or comparison that made an abstract truth accessible through concrete imagery. Ezekiel, Nathan, and Amos all used mashal-forms; rabbinic literature preserved hundreds of meshalim. Jesus’ parables share the form but introduce distinctive elements — particularly the reversal-of-expectations structure and the consistent focus on the kingdom of heaven as the reality being described. The parable of the sower opens the discourse with an agricultural scene familiar to every peasant farmer in Galilee.
4: Palestinian agricultural practice typically involved broadcast sowing (scattering seed widely) before plowing it under, rather than the reverse. This means the sower deliberately sowed on paths, rocky ground, and among thorns before the field was prepared — the seed would land on all types of ground before any selective plowing. The path (hē hodos) alongside or through a field was hard-packed by foot traffic; seed falling there would lie on the surface and be immediately available to birds.
5: Rocky ground (ta petrōdē) is a characteristic feature of Galilean terrain: a thin layer of soil over bedrock limestone. Such soil warms quickly in spring (enabling fast germination) but has no depth for the root system to develop, and dries out rapidly in summer heat. The visual reality of green shoots springing up quickly in such soil, then withering as the ground dried, was an annually observable agricultural event for Galilean farmers.
6: The scorching sun (hēliou anateilantos ekauma\-tisthē) in the Palestinian summer (June-August) could reach temperatures over 40‰C (104‰F). Shallow-rooted plants growing in thin soil over limestone had no water reserve to draw on during the hot season. The agricultural reality was stark: without adequate root depth, the same rapid growth that characterized shallow-soil plants became a death sentence once the summer heat arrived.
7: Thorns and thorn-bushes (akanthai) were ubiquitous in Palestinian fields. The most common thorny weed was the Syrian thistle (Notobasis syriaca) or the bramble (Rubus ulmifolius). These grew vigorously and could overtop and choke grain crops within the same season. Fallowed ground that had not been thoroughly plowed might contain dormant thorn seeds or roots that would compete with deliberately sown grain.
8: Good soil producing hundredfold, sixtyfold, and thirtyfold reflects realistic but high-yield Palestinian grain harvests. Normal wheat yields in first-century Palestine ranged from 5:1 to Matthew 10:1 (five to ten grains harvested for each sown); yields of 30:1 to 100:1 represent extraordinarily good harvests that would be recognized as exceptional. The descending order (hundred, sixty, thirty) rather than ascending is not a hierarchy of merit but an acknowledgment of variable yield within a category of genuine fruitfulness.
9: "Let the one who has ears hear" — the formula marks teaching that requires interpretive engagement beyond surface hearing. It is used in the Synoptics (Matthew 9:9, 43; Mark 4:9) and Revelation to signal teaching with multiple layers of meaning. In the context of the parable discourse, it signals that the agricultural narrative contains kingdom-meaning that requires active discernment — not everyone who hears the parable will understand what it describes.
10: The disciples’ question — "Why do you speak to them in parables?" — reflects a genuine question about pedagogical strategy. The shift from direct teaching (Sermon on the Mount) to parabolic teaching occurs at the narrative turning point where official opposition (Beelzebul controversy, demand for a sign) has crystallized. Parables functioned in Jesus’ teaching both as vehicles of revelation for receptive hearers and as forms of concealment for those whose spiritual perception had hardened.
11: "To you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven" — the "mysteries" (mystēria) language derives from Jewish apocalyptic literature (Daniel 2:18-19, 27-30; 1 En. 41, 46; 1QpHab Matthew 7:5), where God’s hidden purposes, concealed from the wise of this age, are revealed to the elect. The Dead Sea Scrolls speak extensively of divine razim (mysteries) disclosed to the community through inspired interpretation. Jesus places himself in the role of the apocalyptic revealer who discloses what was hidden.
12: "For to the one who has, more will be given, and he will have an abundance, but from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away" — the principle of accumulative reception reflects the dynamics of knowledge and spiritual perception in any discipline. In Jewish wisdom tradition, wisdom multiplied for those who pursued it and diminished for those who neglected it (cf. Proverbs 1:5; Sir Matthew 18:28). Applied to the kingdom’s mysteries, the principle describes the self-reinforcing nature of receptive hearing: each insight creates capacity for more.
13: "This is why I speak to them in parables, because seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not hear, nor do they understand" — the parable’s simultaneous revelation/concealment function is grounded in the empirical fact of divided response. The same teaching produces understanding in some and incomprehension in others, not because the teaching is deliberately obscure but because the capacity for reception differs. The following citation of Isaiah 6 provides the prophetic framework for this divided response.
14: The citation of Isaiah 6:9-10 — "You will indeed hear but never understand, and you will indeed see but never perceive" — is among the most frequently cited OT passages in the NT (Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27; Romans 11:8). Isaiah 6 is the prophet’s commission: he is sent to a people whose hearts have been made fat, ears heavy, and eyes shut. The NT uses this passage to explain why Jesus’ proclamation met with widespread rejection rather than the universal response one might have expected from the Messiah’s arrival.
15: Isaiah 6’s language of the fat heart (pachyntē hē kardia tou laou toutou), heavy ears, and shut eyes describes a condition of voluntary and judicial hardening: the people have closed their own perception, and God has confirmed that closure as judgment. The sequence — "lest they perceive... and turn, and I would heal them" — frames the hardening as mercy’s opposite: those who would not return receive the confirmation of their refusal. Matthew applies this to the generation that witnessed Jesus’ signs and rejected them.
16: "But blessed are your eyes, for they see, and your ears, for they hear" — the Beatitude is addressed to disciples who are presented as experiencing what the prophets longed for but did not receive. The contrast with the hardened crowd is not about the disciples’ inherent superiority but about the gift of receptive perception that they have received: they are in the position of those to whom the mysteries are given (Matthew 13:11).
17: "Truly, I say to you, many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see, and did not see it, and to hear what you hear, and did not hear it" — the prophets’ longing for the eschatological fulfillment they announced was a recognized theme in Jewish thought. The Qumran community explicitly stated that the prophets did not know the full meaning of what they prophesied, which was being revealed to the community in their time (1QpHab Matthew 7:1-5). The disciples are presented as living in the age the prophets could only anticipate.
18: The transition "hear then the parable of the sower" moves from the metadiscourse about parables to the explanation of the specific parable. The explanation was addressed to the disciples privately (Matthew 13:36: "he left the crowds and went into the house"), distinguishing between the public parable (heard by all) and the private explanation (given to the receptive inner circle). This pattern of public proclamation and private instruction is characteristic of the Markan/Matthean Jesus.
19: "The evil one comes and snatches away what has been sown in his heart" — the path-seed represents those who hear the word but have no internal reception space; it remains on the surface where the adversary removes it. In Jewish demonology, the "evil one" (ho ponēros) was the personal opponent of God’s purposes; the Qumran texts described the "spirit of darkness" actively opposing the truth. The immediate passive removal of the word describes a hearing without any inner engagement.
20: The rocky ground represents those who receive the word with immediate enthusiasm (meta charas = with joy) but have no root. In first-century terms, this describes the typical response to charismatic popular movements: initial enthusiastic crowds who follow on the basis of excitement but lack the depth of commitment to survive opposition. The crowds who acclaimed Jesus on Palm Sunday and abandoned him before the Passion may illustrate exactly this pattern.
21: "Tribulation or persecution arises on account of the word" — the social cost of discipleship in the first-century context was real and varied: synagogue exclusion, family division (cf. Matthew 10:35-36), loss of economic relationships, and in some cases legal jeopardy. The shallow-rooted hearer "immediately falls away" (euthys skandalizetai) — the Passion narrative’s scattering of the disciples is itself an illustration of this pattern at the level of the Twelve.
22: The thorny ground represents those whose word-reception is choked by "the cares of the age and the deceitfulness of riches" (hē merimna tou aiōnos kai hē apatē tou ploutou). "Cares of the age" points to the economic anxieties of subsistence-level peasant life: concern about food, clothing, shelter — the survival concerns Jesus addressed in the Sermon (Matthew 6:25-34). "Deceitfulness of riches" points to the other end: wealth that promises security but substitutes mammon-anxiety for kingdom-trust. Both are described as thorns that choke without appearing to — the grain looks normal until it never produces.
23: The good soil that bears fruit in varying amounts (hundredfold, sixty, thirty) represents the range of productive reception, not a hierarchy of merit. Jewish Wisdom literature described fruitful reception of instruction as the goal of formation: "plant wisdom in yourself and you will reap it in abundance" (Sir Matthew 6:19-20 LXX). The variable yields (100, 60, 30) acknowledge that genuine fruitfulness takes many forms and magnitudes — the criterion is production, not uniformity.
24: The parable of the weeds (zizania) is unique to Matthew and addressed to the crowds, followed by private explanation for the disciples (Matthew 13:36-43). The sowing of weeds in an enemy’s field was a recognized act of agricultural sabotage in antiquity: Digest 9.2.27.14 (Roman law) mentions sowing weeds in another’s crop as a specific legal offense. The enemy who "came and sowed weeds among the wheat and went away" describes a nocturnal sabotage act — real enough to be legally categorized in Roman law.
25: The specific weed described is zizanion — identified with Lolium temulentum (darnel), a grass indistinguishable from wheat in its early growth stages. Its roots intertwine with wheat roots at the seedling stage; early removal would uproot both. The grain of darnel is a small dark seed that is mildly toxic (containing the fungal alkaloid temuline) and was a genuine agricultural problem in ancient Palestinian grain fields. Only at harvest, when the full heads of grain vs. the narrow darnel seed appear, can they be reliably distinguished and separated.
26: When the grain appeared (blastan = sprouted), the darnel (zizania) also appeared — visually distinct for the first time by the character of their grain. The servants’ discovery of the mixed field at this point reflects the agricultural reality: early in the season, you could not know; at grain-head stage, the contamination becomes visible. The parable exploits this agricultural fact to describe the mixed nature of the present kingdom community.
27: The servants’ question — "Master, did you not sow good seed in your field? Where then did the weeds come from?" — reflects the natural management question for a Palestinian farmer: is this natural contamination (cross-pollination, wind-blown seeds) or deliberate sabotage? The master’s answer ("an enemy has done this") identifies the source as intentional hostility rather than accident, activating the legal framework of agricultural sabotage.
28: The servants’ proposed solution — "Do you want us to go and gather them?" — reflects the natural response: immediate removal. The master’s refusal is counter-intuitive from a farming perspective (darnel infestation worsens each year) but is grounded in the practical reality of root entanglement and the parable’s theological purpose: premature judgment would destroy the wheat along with the weeds.
29: "No, lest in gathering the weeds you root up the wheat along with them" — the agricultural logic is sound for the seedling stage where root systems are intertwined, but at harvest the plants are mature and can be individually cut. The delayed separation until harvest maps the eschatological judgment: the present age is not the time for definitive separation of the righteous and the wicked; that is the prerogative of the harvesters (angels) at the end of the age.
30: The harvest command — "gather the weeds first and bind them in bundles to be burned, but gather the wheat into my barn" — reverses the order from agricultural practice (normally good grain gathered first) to emphasize the priority of removing the weeds. Darnel straw was usable as fuel; it was typically bundled separately from wheat for burning in bread ovens or lime kilns. The agricultural detail supports the theological point: what was mixed and indistinguishable during growth is definitively separated and differently disposed of at harvest.
31: The mustard seed (kokkō sinapeos) was proverbially the smallest seed in Jewish tradition (m. Niddah Matthew 5:2: "the smallest of all seeds"). The specific species is likely Sinapis alba (white mustard) or Sinapis nigra (black mustard), both common in Palestinian fields and gardens. While the seed is small, the plant grows to 8-10 feet (2.5-3 meters) in Palestinian climate — unusually large for what is technically an annual herb rather than a tree.
32: "The birds of the air come and make nests in its branches" — in the OT, birds nesting in a large tree is a standard image for the nations finding shelter under the protection of a great empire (Daniel 4:12, 21 uses it for Nebuchadnezzar’s Babylon; Ezekiel 17:23 for Israel’s restoration; 31:6 for Egypt). The Gentile nations finding shelter in the mustard-tree kingdom was an image the Jewish audience would have recognized as pointing beyond Israel to an inclusive scope. The kingdom starts invisibly small (one itinerant teacher) and grows to encompass the nations.
33: Leaven (zymē) in Jewish culture was predominantly a negative symbol: leavened bread was forbidden during Passover (Exodus 12:15-20; Matthew 13:7); leaven was a metaphor for sin and corruption in Pauline usage (1 Corinthians 5:6-8; Galatians 5:9). The parable’s use of leaven as a positive image for the kingdom’s transformation is therefore surprising and possibly deliberately subversive of conventional associations. Three measures (sata tria) of meal equaled approximately 40 liters — enough for a large communal baking (Abraham’s hospitality in Genesis 18:6 also uses three measures, a possible allusion).
34: "Jesus said all these things to the crowds in parables; indeed, he said nothing to them without a parable" — Matthew’s summary statement generalizes the parabolic teaching method as characteristic of Jesus’ public discourse at this point in his ministry. This functions as a narrative note explaining the discourse structure: the crowds receive parables, the disciples receive explanations. The statement sets up the following citation of Psalms 78:2.
35: The citation of Psalms 78:2 — "I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter what has been hidden since the foundation of the world" — identifies Jesus’ parabolic teaching as the fulfillment of Asaph’s promise. Psalm 78 is a historical psalm (maskil of Asaph) retelling Israel’s covenant history from the Exodus through David; the promise to "speak in parables" refers to making hidden wisdom accessible through narrative form. Matthew’s "hidden since the foundation of the world" expands the psalm’s scope to the primordial — what Jesus reveals in parables is not merely Israel’s history but the cosmos’ foundational purposes.
36: Jesus’ return to the house (apheis tous ochlous elthen eis tēn oikian) creates the private instruction setting for the disciples. The house was the standard Jewish teaching venue for intimate instruction; rabbinic schools met in the teacher’s house. The distinction between public teaching (parables to crowds) and private explanation (house for disciples) structured Jesus’ pedagogical practice — the disciples were being formed as the community that would transmit the explanations.
37: "The one who sows the good seed is the Son of Man" — the allegorical explanation identifies each element of the parable precisely. The Son of Man as sower positions Jesus’ ministry as the act that introduces the kingdom mixture into the world. The allegorical method here is closer to the pesher interpretation of the Dead Sea Scrolls (1QpHab) than to typical rabbinic parable: each element has a one-to-one correspondence with a reality in the eschatological drama.
38: "The field is the world" — notably not "the field is Israel" or "the field is the church." The parable operates on the cosmic scale: the mixed community of righteous and wicked exists throughout the kosmos, not just within one nation or community. "The sons of the kingdom" and "the sons of the evil one" are technical designations from apocalyptic dualism (similar to Qumran’s "sons of light" and "sons of darkness," 1QS Matthew 3:13-4:26) — they describe fundamental orientation rather than moral perfection.
39: "The harvest is the end of the age, and the reapers are angels" — the harvest as a metaphor for eschatological judgment has deep OT roots (Joel 3:13; Isaiah 27:12; Jeremiah 51:33). The angels as eschatological agents of judgment appears in Jewish apocalyptic literature (1 En. Matthew 56:1-4; Matthew 63:1) and throughout Revelation. The end of the age (synteleia tou aiōnos) is the technical term for the eschatological transition from the present age to the coming age — a concept central to Second Temple Jewish eschatology.
40: The burning of the darnel at the end of the age activates the standard apocalyptic fire-of-judgment imagery. Fire as the agent of eschatological purification/destruction appears throughout the prophets (Isaiah 66:15-16; Malachi 4:1; Zephaniah 1:18) and Jewish apocalyptic texts. The "furnace of fire" (kaminos tou pyros) is a specific image that recalls Dan 3’s furnace — though there the righteous were preserved in the fire rather than the wicked burned by it.
41: "The Son of Man will send his angels, and they will gather out of his kingdom all causes of sin and all law-breakers" — the eschatological kingdom gathering in reverse: rather than gathering the righteous in, the angels gather out the causes of stumbling (skandala) and those who practice lawlessness (anomia). The phrase "causes of sin" (panta ta skandala) refers to the things and people that cause others to fall — a category that includes both deceiving teachers and systemic corruptions of the community.
42: "The furnace of fire" with "weeping and gnashing of teeth" (klauthnos kai brygmos tōn odontōn) — the weeping/gnashing formula appears six times in Matthew (Matthew 8:12; Matthew 13:42, 50; Matthew 22:13; Matthew 24:51; Matthew 25:30) and once in Luke (Matthew 13:28), functioning as Matthew’s standard description of eschatological suffering. "Gnashing of teeth" in Jewish texts (Psalms 112:10; Lamentations 2:16) expresses rage, anguish, or despair. The combination describes the intense emotional state of those experiencing the consequences of their choices.
43: "Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father" echoes Daniel 12:3 ("those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the sky above, and those who turn many to righteousness, like the stars forever and ever"). The Daniel reference locates the promise within the resurrection and vindication tradition of Jewish apocalyptic: those who endured faithfully through persecution and hardening will be publicly vindicated and transformed in eschatological glory.
44: The hidden treasure parable draws on the common ancient near-eastern practice of burying valuables — coins, jewelry, family wealth — in clay jars in one’s field as protection against theft, raiding, or political upheaval. Palestine’s turbulent history (Babylonian conquest, Ptolemaic-Seleucid conflicts, Roman occupation) meant that the sudden death of a property-owner could leave buried valuables undiscovered for generations. Archaeological excavations in Israel regularly uncover coin hoards from precisely this kind of emergency burial. The finder of such a hoard had a windfall claim if he acted quickly and legally.
45: The pearl merchant (emporos = traveling merchant, wholesale dealer) who seeks fine pearls represents a different social type from the field-discoverer: a professional whose entire economic life is organized around acquiring and trading pearls. Pearls in the ancient world came primarily from the Persian Gulf (Bahrain), the Red Sea, and the Indian Ocean; they were among the most valued luxury commodities of the Roman world, fetching extraordinary prices. First-century Rome had a pearl craze: Pliny (Natural History 9.54-58) records individual pearls worth millions of sesterces.
46: "On finding one pearl of great value, he went and sold all that he had and bought it" — the merchant’s action of liquidating his entire inventory to acquire one supreme pearl was a recognizable business logic: if you find something worth more than everything else you possess, rational self-interest demands conversion. The parable does not romanticize the merchant’s decision as sacrifice; it presents it as the obvious rational response to the discovery of supreme value. The kingdom similarly demands total reorientation because it is genuinely worth more than any alternative.
47: The dragnet (sagēnē) was a large seine or drag net used for commercial fishing — different from the smaller cast-net (amphiblestron) used by Peter and Andrew. A drag net was a large mesh bag weighted at the bottom and floated at the top that was drawn through the water (sometimes by two boats) to capture everything in its path. The Sea of Galilee supported a significant commercial fishing industry; first-century excavations at Magdala (Tarichaeae) have found evidence of large fish-processing operations. The dragnet’s indiscriminate capture ("of every kind") is the parable’s key agricultural-economic fact.
48: Sorting the catch on the shore (kathisantes syneléxan ta kala eis aggeē ta de sapra exō ebalon) reflected both economic necessity and Jewish purity law. Commercially, different species had different market values; inedible or damaged fish were culled. Religiously, Leviticus 11:9-12 and Deuteronomy 14:9-10 defined clean fish (having fins and scales) and unclean fish (those lacking them) — catfish, eels, and other scaleless species were ritually impure and could not be sold to observant Jews. The sorting was both commercial grading and ritual classification.
49: "So it will be at the end of the age" — the parable explicitly claims eschatological significance: the present mixed community (like the dragnet’s catch) will be definitively sorted at the end of the age by angels. The parable’s context (after the weeds parable and its explanation) underlines the consistency of the theme: the kingdom’s present reality is mixed and incomplete; its eschatological reality will be purified and definitive.
50: The "furnace of fire" and "weeping and gnashing of teeth" repeat the weeds parable’s eschatological closing (Matthew 13:42) — a rhetorical anaphora that reinforces the twin parables’ shared conclusion. The repetition signals that this is not incidental imagery but the core claim: the same world that appears mixed and undifferentiated now will be precisely and definitively sorted then. The emotional description of the wicked’s final state functions as a warning rather than as theological precision about the nature of eschatological punishment.
51: "Have you understood all these things?" — Jesus’ direct question to the disciples after the private explanations tests their comprehension and completion of the parabolic instruction. The disciples’ "yes" is a narrative statement of their having received the inside knowledge — in contrast to the crowds who heard but did not understand. The question also introduces the final parable of the scribe trained for the kingdom.
52: The scribe trained for the kingdom of heaven (grammateus mathēteutheis tē̄ basileia̧ tōn ouranōn) who brings out from his storehouse (thēsaurou) both new and old describes the ideal disciple-transmitter. The scribe was the professional custodian and interpreter of written texts in ancient Jewish life — the one who read, copied, and explained Torah and the prophetic scrolls. A scribe "discipled for the kingdom" combines the traditioned learning of Jewish scribal culture with the new kingdom-perspective Jesus has been teaching. Matthew’s community, which carefully preserved Jesus’ teaching in writing, is implicitly this kind of scribe.
53: The closing formula "when Jesus had finished these parables" (hote etelesen ho Iēsous tas parabolois tautas) is the third of the five discourse-closing formulas in Matthew (Matthew 7:28; Matthew 11:1; Matthew 13:53; Matthew 19:1; Matthew 26:1). The five-discourse structure mirrors the Pentateuch’s five books — a deliberate structural choice that presents Jesus as the new Moses delivering the new Torah. The departure to "his own country" (tēn patrida autou) sets up the Nazareth rejection.
54: Jesus’ return to his hometown (patrida autou = Nazareth) and teaching in their synagogue produced astonishment (exeplēssonto) and offense (eskandalizonto). The astonishment was at the content — "where did this man get this wisdom and these mighty works?" — while the offense was rooted in social memory: they knew his family, his trade, and his ordinary origins. In a village social system where one’s identity was entirely determined by family lineage and local reputation, claims to extraordinary authority from a known local were socially intolerable.
55: "Is not this the carpenter’s son?" — the identification of Jesus as a craftsman’s son (tou tektonos huios) is socially precise. The tektōn (craftsman/carpenter/builder) was a skilled artisan of modest social status — above landless day-laborers but below landowners and merchants. His mother and brothers (James, Joseph, Simon, Judas) were all known members of the Nazareth community. The naming of Mary and the brothers is not merely genealogical detail but social identification: "We know these people — how can he be more than he appears to be?"
56: The sisters (hai adelphai autou) are mentioned without being named, consistent with the lower social visibility of women in public discourse. Their presence in Nazareth suggests that Jesus’ family’s base remained in Nazareth while Jesus operated from Capernaum. The comprehensive identification of Jesus’ family network in Matthew 13:55-56 underscores the social impossibility (in their framework) of attributing extraordinary authority to someone so thoroughly known and ordinary.
57: "A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown and in his own household" — the proverb was recognized in the ancient world as a general social observation: familiarity breeds contempt for extraordinary gifts. Epictetus (Discourses 3.16.11) and other Hellenistic writers use similar observations. In the Jewish context, the prophets consistently faced rejection from their own communities (Jeremiah 11:21; Matthew 12:6; Amos 7:10-13). The proverb frames the Nazareth rejection within a recognized pattern of prophetic experience.
58: "He did not do many mighty works there, because of their unbelief" — the connection between unbelief and limited healings is not presented as a mechanical limitation on divine power but as a covenantal dynamic: the healing miracles functioned within a relationship of faith-response; their absence when that relationship was absent reflected the nature of the kingdom’s operation. The healings in Matthew consistently involve faith (Matthew 9:22, 29; Matthew 15:28); their absence in Nazareth reflects the absence of the trust that constituted receptive participation in kingdom restoration.
